coredata.dc:title: Luminescent properties and energy transfer of luminescent carbon dots assembled mesoporous Al2O3: Eu3+ co-doped materials for temperature sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: He, Youling
He, Jiangling
Zhang, Haoran
Liu, Yingliang
Lei, Bingfu
coredata.dc:description: Owning to the hydrogen-band interactions, blue-light-emitting luminescent carbon dots (CDs) synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal treatment were successfully assembled into Eu3+ doped mesoporous aluminas (MAs). Interesting, dual-emissive CDs/MAs co-doped materials with higher quantum yield (QY), long-term stability, mesoporous structure, high thermal stability, and large surface areas were obtained. Furthermore, the obtained CDs/MAs co-doped materials possessed tunable color, and excellent temperature sensitivity due to the existing of energy transfer between CDs and Eu3+ ion. The energy transfer efficiency (η) and energy transfer probability (P) for CDs/Eu3+ co-doped materials possessed a monotonous tendency with the change of Eu3+ content. More importantly, the dual-emissive colors can be regularly adjusted through regulating their excitation wavelength or relative mass ratio. In addition, the emission intensity of the CDs/MAs co-doped materials gradually decreased with increasing temperature showing the clear temperature dependence, this dual-emissive thermometer was with high sensitivity, owning a great fitted curve in the range from 100 to 360K under a single wavelength excitation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescent carbon dots
Mesoporous aluminas
Temperature sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the CDs/MAs co-doped materials
TEM, HRTEM, FT-IR spectra, XPS spectrum
The mechanism analysis of the color-tunable CDs/MAs materials

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 51203053, 21571067), the Guangzhou Science & Technology Project (No. 201605112017209), the Project for Construction of High-level University in Guangdong Province, and the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (b) TEM images of MAs. (c) TEM image of CDs coating from aqueous solution. (d) TEM image of the CDs/MAs and HRTEM image of a carbon dot (inset). (e) XPS spectrum of the MAs. (f) FTIR spectra of MAs, CDs/MAs, and the CDs.
(a) Wide-angle and (b) small angle XRD patterns of as-synthesized MAs, and CDs/MAs with mass ratios of 0.5%, 1.0%, 2.0%, 3.0%, and 4.0%, respectively.
(a) Pore-size distribution curves and (b) nitrogen absorption-desorption isotherms of MAs and CDs/MAs (0.5%, 1.0%, 2.0%, 3.0%, and 4.0%), respectively.

(a) Excitation (Ι, em: 616nm) and emission spectra (ΙΙ, ex: 395nm) of MAs. (b) Excitation-dependence spectra of CDs aqueous solution with excitation wavelength ranging from 280 to 500nm, and excitation spectrum of CDs aqueous solution monitored at 450nm. (c) Emission spectra for CDs/MAs (mass ratio: 4.0%) under excitation wavelength from 340 to 430nm. (d) Normalized fluorescent spectra of MAs (excited at 395nm), CDs (excited at 360nm), and CDs/MAs (mass ratio: 4.0%, excited at 360nm). (e) Photos of the CDs solution under daylight (left) and UV light of 365nm (right). (f) CIE chromaticity diagram for CDs/MAs (mass ratio: 4.0%) with increasing excitation wavelength from 340 to 430nm, recorded every 10nm.

(a) Excitation spectrum of the MAs and the emission spectrum of the CDs solution. (b) Emission spectra of the CDs/MAs samples were excited at 360nm in 1.0mgCDs/1mL, 2mL, 3mL, 4mL 0.5molL−1 Eu(NO3)3, respectively. (c) Decay curves of the CDs 425nm emission in 1.0mgCDs/0mL, 1mL, 2mL, 3mL, 4mL 0.5molL−1 Eu(NO3)3 co-doped hybrid materials at 360nm excitation, respectively. (d) CIE chromaticity diagram for a CDs aqueous solution, pure MAs co-doped materials, and CDs/MAs samples with different mass ratio, whereby samples 1 to 4 refer to 1.0mgCDs/1mL, 2mL, 3mL, 4mL 0.5molL−1 Eu(NO3)3, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs/MAs (mass ratio: 4.0%) recorded every 20K from 100K to 360K. The sample was excited at 360nm. (b) The ratio of MAs (616nm) over CDs (425nm) peaks as a function of temperature were plotted for CDs/MAs (mass ratio: 4.0%) and the fitted curve for the co-doped materials.
Preparation process of CDs/MAs hybrid materials.
Dependence of the CDs single-doped lifetime and CDs/MAs co-doped sampled energy transfer efficiency in 1.0mgCDs/0mL, 1mL, 2mL, 3mL, 4mL 0.5molL−1 Eu(NO3)3 co-doped materials.

head.ce:title: Luminescent properties and energy transfer of luminescent carbon dots assembled mesoporous Al2O3: Eu3+ co-doped materials for temperature sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owning to the hydrogen-band interactions, blue-light-emitting luminescent carbon dots (CDs) synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal treatment were successfully assembled into Eu3+ doped mesoporous aluminas (MAs). Interesting, dual-emissive CDs/MAs co-doped materials with higher quantum yield (QY), long-term stability, mesoporous structure, high thermal stability, and large surface areas were obtained. Furthermore, the obtained CDs/MAs co-doped materials possessed tunable color, and excellent temperature sensitivity due to the existing of energy transfer between CDs and Eu3+ ion. The energy transfer efficiency (η) and energy transfer probability (P) for CDs/Eu3+ co-doped materials possessed a monotonous tendency with the change of Eu3+ content. More importantly, the dual-emissive colors can be regularly adjusted through regulating their excitation wavelength or relative mass ratio. In addition, the emission intensity of the CDs/MAs co-doped materials gradually decreased with increasing temperature showing the clear temperature dependence, this dual-emissive thermometer was with high sensitivity, owning a great fitted curve in the range from 100 to 360K under a single wavelength excitation.

ce:section.ce:para: Alumina is a proverbial industry catalyst, possessing abundant predominant thermal stability and Lewis-acid sites firstly. Compared with conventional mesoporous solid materials, mesoporous aluminas provided relative large surface areas, high thermal stability, and narrow pore distribution over a wide range [1]. Based on the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), mesoporous materials are classified as porous materials whether pore sizes between 2nm and 50nm [2]. Much research efforts so far have been focused on mesostructures materials, Schlenker’s work were first synthesized the ordered mesoporous silica materials in 1992 [3]. Soon after, mesoporous aluminas have been attracted importance in this research field. Yang et al. [4] has reported on mesoporous aluminas, firstly using block copolymers as templates. Notably, Yan’s team presented an easily accessible, high-throughput, and reproducible sol-gel strategy to successfully synthesize highly ordered mesoporous aluminas [5]. Consequently, as a result of their superior properties, mesoporous aluminas are in accordance with promising carrier candidates for numerous exciting applications, such as temperature sensors, and support of catalysts.

Temperature is a fundamental physiological property that affects the process of chemical and biological systems [6,7]. Unfortunately, traditional temperature sensors generally based on the invasive probe by testing heat flow, its optimal sensitive and accurate cannot detect temperature of small confined spaces simultaneously. Considerable research efforts have been devoted to fluorescence-based temperature measurements owning to their broad potential applications in sensor technology, color display, microfluidic, and vivo imaging [8–10]. However, it is still a challenge to obtain of the fluorescence-based temperature sensing with synthesized simple and high sensitivity in wide temperature range [11–13]. Luminescent materials refer to semiconductor nanocrystals, carbon dots (CDs), organic fluorescent dyes, and rare-earth ions [14,15]. Specially, luminescent CDs occupied a unique position in fluorescent carbon materials, have been attracted widespread attention such as WLED-yields, and temperature sensing because of their stability, non-toxicity, and environment friendly [16–19]. Thus, RE ions or CDs doped MAs can be considered to be the potential optical materials. CDs have significant self-quenching in the aggregation state, which hamper their applications [20,21]. Noteworthy to mention that few researches focus on the CDs/RE ions co-doped materials, it is essential to discover an ideal the assembled materials.

In this work, we first synthesized highly ordered mesoporous aluminas of assembled triply ionized europium (MAs) through the sol-gel route. The MAs possessed high thermal stability, large surface areas, and turned pore sizes. Simultaneously, the water-soluble nitrogen-doped CDs were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal treatment. Interestingly, based on the hydrogen-band interaction, CDs can be successfully assembled into MAs by a simple stirring process. In optical properties and application, the co-doped materials exhibited the tunable color through regulating their excitation wavelength or relative mass ratio, and high sensitive temperature sensing in the range from 100 to 360K under a single wavelength excitation. Moreover, the mechanism of energy transfer between CDs and Eu3+ ion are displayed and discussed systematically. The work opens new avenues for temperature-sensing materials.

De-ionized water was used in this work. Tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethylenediamine (EDA), diethylenetriamine (DETA), acetone, and dimethyl formamide (DMF) were purchased from Guangdong Guanghua Technology Co. Ltd. These organic solvents are analytical solvents. The degree of polymerization of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is 1750±50 (molecular weight: 77,000±2200). Pluronic P123 (EO20PO70EO20, Maverage =5800) was purchased from Macklin Ltd. Aluminum isopropoxide (99.99%) and Eu2O3 (99.99%) were purchased from Guangzhou Taiwei Co. Ltd. Glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH), anhydrous citric acid (99.5%, AR), hydrochloric acid (HCl), nitric acid (HNO3), ethanol (99.7%) were purchased from Guangzhou Longke Co. Ltd.

According to a reported method [5], 2.0g of Pluronic P123, 1.0g of anhydrous citric acid were dissolved in 30mL of ethanol with vigorous stirring, and transparent liquid was obtained. Then, 2.0mL of 37 (wt.) hydrochloric acid and 3.0mL of glacial acetic acid were added. Then, 20mmol of aluminum is-propoxide was dissolved into the mixture with vigorous stirring at room temperature for 12h. Simultaneously, 0.50mL of 0.50molL−1 Eu(NO3)3 was gradually dropped with vigorous stirring for 5–10h, then the prepared sample was transferred to a water bath at 40°C by stirring for 5h. The obtained white sol was placed in an atmosphere of 60°C to undergo solvent evaporation. After 48h, white solid-state powders were collected. The calcination process was carried out by slowly increasing the temperature from room temperature to 500°C (a ramping rate of 1°Cmin−1), and then heating at 500°C for 5h in air. After that, a high-temperature treatment at 800°C was carried out in air for 2h with a temperature ramp of 10°Cmin−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the CDs/MAs co-doped materials
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots (CDs) were prepared based on a recent study [22]. Scheme 1 reveals the preparation process of the CDs/MAs co-doped materials. In a typical procedure, 1.0g of MAs was mixed with different amounts of CDs, the mass ratio of CDs/MAs was regulated to 0.5%, 1.0%, 2.0%, 3.0% and 4.0%. After simple stirring for 8h, the co-doped materials were centrifuged for 15min (5000rpmmin−1). The obtained precipitation was filtered with a Buchner funnel and dried at 333K in an oven.

Crystal structure of materials was collected by small angle powder X-ray diffraction (SAXRD, Rigaku) with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5418Å). The data were collected in a 2θ range from 0.6° to 4° at a scanning step of 0.02° and a scanning rate of 0.2° min−1. The particle morphology was analyzed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) and a high resolution TEM (TECNAI12, Holland), a small amount of the sample were dispersed into deionized water during sample preparation. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with an ultraviolet–visible spectrofluorometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu). The absolute photoluminescence (PL) quantum yields (QYs) were carried out the same spectrometer with an integrating sphere with its inner face coated with BENFLEC. Nitrogen physisorption isotherms were analysed employing a Micromeritics ASAP 2020HD88 system. The infrared spectra were adopted on a Nicolet Avatar 360 Fourier transformation infrared spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere, using KBr as a reference. The pore size distributions were calculated from the discrete Fourier transform (DFT). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a monochromator (resolution: 0.2nm) and a 150W Xe lamp as the excitation source. Temperature-dependent photoluminescence emission spectra were conducted by combining a heating apparatus (Oxford Instruments) with the Hitachi F-7000. Detailedly, the sable temperature atmosphere was provided by a heating apparatus, then, the correspondent photoluminescence emission spectrum was measured by the Hitachi F-7000. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was monitored on a Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD. The fluorescence decay curves were tested on a FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM, HRTEM, FT-IR spectra, XPS spectrum
ce:section.ce:para: As demonstrated by the transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) in Fig. 1 , the microscopic morphologies structures of the MAs, the CDs, and the CDs/MAs co-doped materials have been clear observed. Detailedly, Fig.1(a) and (b) display the wormhole mesoporous structures, and uniform ordered pore with a pore size in a range from 10 to 12nm with worm-like pore morphology [23]. CDs dispersed in deionized water with uniform spherical particles, and the average sizes are approximately 5nm, as shown in Fig.1(c). A TEM image of the CDs/MAs phosphors presents several CD-like dots with pore sizes in the range of 4 to 5nm in Fig.1(d). For further validation, a closer observation [inset in Fig.1(d)] shows high crystallinity with a lattice fringe distance of around 0.22nm, corresponding to that of the (100) facet of graphitic carbon [24]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the MAs additionally confirm that the MAs have been successfully prepared in Fig.1(e). Europium is monitored in the MAs by Eu3d3/2, Eu3d5/2, and Eu4d signals, composed of three peaks at 1180eV, 1170eV, and 120eV, respectively [25].

In line with the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the CDs, MAs, and CDs/MAs phosphors, Fig.1(f) reveals the existence of abundant -OH and -NH2 due to the strong and broad absorption bands at 3300–3400cm−1 and 1000–1200cm−1 [22]. By summarizing the characterization data and analyses, the CDs/MAs phosphors are synthesized owning to the hydrogen band.

The wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of MAs and CDs/MAs co-doped materials indicate only one weak amorphous diffraction peak at approximately 20°, as shown in Fig.2 (a), attributed to ordered mesostructure [26]. Fig.2(b) shows that the small-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the MAs and the CDs/MAs. We observe an obvious diffraction peak at around 0.5°C, presenting a ordered mesostructure [27]. Therefore, it is a conclusion that the mesostructured aluminas can be drew from the wide-angel and the small-angel (XRD).

Narrow pore-size distribution curves of MAs and CDs/MAs is observed from Fig.3 (a). Narrow pore-size distributions are retained in a range from 10 to 15nm [28]. Table S1 summarizes the BET surface area, pore size, and pore volume of MAs and the CDs/MAs co-doped materials with different mass ratios (0.5%, 1.0%, 2.0%, 3.0%, and 4.0%). The obtained pure MAs exhibit a large BET surface areas of 271.84m2 g−1, a wide pore volume of 0.71cm3 g−1, and a narrow pore size of 14.94nm. After adding CDs increasingly, the BET surface area, pore size, and pore volume are apparently decreased, coinciding with that CDs can be assembled into MAs. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of MAs and CDs/MAs are denoted type-IV curves with H1-shaped hysteresis loops in Fig.3(b), representing their mesostructures and homogeneous cylindrical pores [29].

Luminescent properties will be further discussed below. On the basis of the relevant literature [30], experimental data indicate that the quantum yield (QY) of the CDs solution is approximately 33%, the QY of the MAs is around 2.11%, and QY of the CDs/MAs is around 46.69%. The co-doped materials maintain more than one month the initial fluorescence, as shown in Fig. S1. The CDs/MAs co-doped materials are synthesized with the higher QY and long-term stability. As can be seen from Fig.4 (a), the characteristic emission (I) and excitation (II) spectra of the CDs/MAs co-doped materials with 395-nm excitation and 616-nm emission wavelengths respectively. MAs exhibit three typical emission bands under 395nm excitation situated in respectively 591nm, 616nm, and 656nm owning to the energy level transition of Eu3+ [31]. In addition, Fig.4(b) demonstrates the excitation-wavelength-dependent PL spectra of the CDs aqueous solution with the strongest emission peak at 425nm under 360-nm excitation wavelength. This is consistent with the observation from Fig.4(e), the CDs solution emits blue light under UV light of 365nm. The excitation-wavelength-dependent property of CDs is based on a previously reported mechanism [22], attributed to the combination of graphitizing cores, surface fluorophores, and limited excitation-independent fluorophores [32]. Expectantly, as shown in Fig.4(c), the excitation-wavelength-dependent PL spectra of the CDs/MAs co-doped materials display the characteristic emission peaks both of CDs and MAs under a single excitation wavelength from 340nm to 430nm in intervals of 10nm. The emission intensity of the CDs/MAs co-doped materials can be systematically turned, the characteristic emission peaks of CDs are regularly red shift, and the characteristic emission peaks of the MAs are not changed. Under 360-nm excitation wavelength, the characteristic peaks of the MAs corresponding to the CDs/MAs are not changed, but the characteristic peaks of the CDs are blue shift in Fig.4(d). The results are also in good consistent with the result that the CDs are assembled into the MAs, furthermore, the energy transfer can be existed between CDs and Eu3+ ion, complete details of this casewillbe provided at alatertime. Naturally, the emission color of the co-doped materials is easily controlled through adjusting their excitation wavelength. Identical conclusions were obtained in Fig.4(f). In accordance of the CIE chromaticity coordinates with various excitation wavelengths from 340 to 430nm, the colors of these footprints pass from blue to white-light region, and then from white to green-light region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism analysis of the color-tunable CDs/MAs materials
ce:section.ce:para:
To gain the mechanism of the color-tunable CDs/MAs co-doped materials, Fig.5 (a) reveals that the excitation spectrum of the MAs and the emission spectrum of the CDs at 360nm excitation wavelength have partly overlap [33]. Furthermore, the PL spectra of 1.0mg CDs/1mL, 2mL, 3mL, 4mL 0.5molL−1 Eu(NO3)3 co-doped materials under the 360nm excitation are showed in Fig.5(b). It can be observed that the peak intensity of Eu3+ ion monotonously enhances with increasing concentration of Eu3+ ion, the luminescent intensity of the CDs weakens gradually. In accordance with the CIE chromaticity coordinates in Fig.5(d), the six colors footprints represent the pure CDs solution, the CDs/MAs from 1 to 4 with different relative concentrations (1.0mg CDs/1mL, 2mL, 3mL, 4mL 0.5molL−1 Eu(NO3)3, respectively) and pure MAs, respectively. Conclusively, the six colors footprints are regularly changed from blue to purplish-blue, and from white to purplish-red. These results reveal that the energy transfer process from the CDs donor to the MAs acceptor might occur [34]. In order to demonstrate the energy transfer process of CDs to Eu3+, the decay curves of 1.0mg CDs/0mL, 1mL, 2mL, 3mL, 4mL 0.5molL−1 Eu(NO3)3 co-doped materials are showed by monitoring CDs under 425-nm emission at 360-nm excitation wavelengths in Fig.5(c). The fluorescence decays become quicker as the increasing of Eu3+ ions content. This evidence is in accord with the occurrence of energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ ion. The fluorescence lifetime of the co-doped materials can be described by the following equation [33]:
(1)
τ =
∫ I ( t ) tdt
∫ I ( t ) dt
where I(t) refers to the luminescent intensity at time. It is fitted by Eq. (1), as displayed in Table 1 . On the basis of these decay profiles, the energy transfer efficiency (η) and the probability (P) of CDs → Eu3+ energy transfer can be calculated by the following equation:
(2)
η = 1 -
τ CDs / MAs
τ CDs
(3)
P =
1
τ CDs / MAs
-
1
τ CDs
where τCDs and τCDs/MAs denote the lifetimes of CDs in the CDs single-doped and CDs/Eu3+ co-doped sampled, respectively. As the increase of the Eu3+ ions concentration from 0mL to 4mL of 0.5molL−1 Eu(NO3)3, the lifetimes of CDs gradually quick from 97.60ns to 80.65ns. The η and P increases monotonously, relevant lifetime values are definitely listed in Table 1.

Generally, the intensity ratio of two characteristic emission peaks is used as the thermometric parameter in ratiometric luminescent temperature sensor [35]. The CDs/MAs co-doped materials are triumphantly suitable for temperature sensing. As shown in Fig.6 (a), the fluorescence spectra of the CDs/MAs are provided from 100 to 360K in intervals of 20K under 360-nm excitation wavelength [36]. The characteristic emission intensities of both CDs at 425nm and MAs at 616nm gradually weaken as temperature increasing. The result is related to non-radiative recombination, which plays an important role in the PL thermal quenching and the activation of non-radiative recombination centers is enhanced with increasing temperature [34]. In addition, the characteristic peaks the CDs are displayed red shift due to the energy transfer of the CDs/Eu3+, which is verified the Fig. 5.

Furthermore, it is defined the ratio of the intensities of the MAs at 616nm and that of the CDs at 425nm (F616/F425 ). As shown in Fig.6(b), the plot of F616/F425 has a great fitted curve from 100K to 360K (R2 =0.993), which can be calculated by following equation [37]:
(4)
F 616 / F 425 = 2.72 - 0.0058 T
where T refers to the temperature in the range of 100–360K. For a ratiometric luminescent temperature sensing, the relative sensitivity can be calculated by following equation:
(5)
S =
∂ ( IA / IB ) / ∂ T
IA / IB
where IA and IB exhibit the luminescence intensities of the dual emission, respectively. The maximum sensitivity of the CDs/MAs is achieved at 360K. In conclusion, the CDs/MAs are triumphantly applied in the temperature sensing as temperature range from 100 to 360K. It is also potted the change in the fluorescence intensity of the 616nm peak as a function of temperature in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity changed linearly in the range of 100–320K (R2 =0.998).

We have demonstrated the systematic fabrication of highly ordered mesoporous aluminas of assembled triply ionized europium (MAs) possessing turned pore size, large surface area, high thermal stability, and emitting red light under UV light. Then, by a simple strategy, the obtained blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) can be successfully dispersed in MAs due to hydrogen-bands interaction, the color-tunable and higher QY CDs/MAs co-doped materials were obtained. Moreover, it has been demonstrated the existence of the energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+, the emissive colors of the CDs/MAs co-doped materials can be regularly adjusted through regulating their excitation wavelengths, or by changing their relative mass ratio. Meanwhile, MAs are with narrow pore size distribution and large surface, revealing their excellent properties in temperature sensing. In fact, the CDs/MAs co-doped materials possess high sensitivity to temperature, owning a great fitted curve in the wide temperature range from 100 to 360K. We propose that this research can open an new avenue in temperature-sensing materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 51203053, 21571067), the Guangzhou Science & Technology Project (No. 201605112017209), the Project for Construction of High-level University in Guangdong Province, and the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.02.013.
